docs.title: adversarial-market-marl
docs.text: A multi-agent reinforcement learning framework for adversarial market microstructure with endogenous information leakage.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This framework models financial markets as adversarial information ecosystems rather than price-discovery mechanisms. Three co-evolving agents contest informational advantage through a realistic limit order book:

Agent Role Objective Execution agent Institutional trader Minimise implementation shortfall and information leakage Market maker Adaptive liquidity provider Maximise spread capture; infer informed vs noise flow Arbitrageur Latency exploiter Profit from belief-update lags

The central question the framework is designed to answer:

To what extent can an informed trader optimally execute large orders while minimising the statistical detectability of their informational advantage?

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Information leakage as a reward signal — mutual information \(I(z; F_t)\) estimated live by a MINE-f neural network and penalised directly in the execution agent's reward
  • Sequential belief inference — Pre-LayerNorm Transformer encoder tracks \(b_t = P(\text{informed} \mid \mathcal{F}_t)\) in real time
  • Endogenous price formation — no exogenous price path; all price discovery emerges from agent interaction
  • Detectability phase transition — infrastructure to locate the critical \(\lambda^*\) where informed flow becomes statistically indistinguishable from noise
  • 222 tests, full CI — black, isort, flake8, mypy, pytest on Python 3.10 and 3.11

docs.title: Quick Install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Nityahapani/adversarial-market-marl.git
cd adversarial-market-marl
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install -e .

See Installation for GPU setup and dependency details.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Smoke test — runs in ~2 minutes on CPU
python scripts/train.py --config configs/fast_debug.yaml --exp-name debug

# Full training run
python scripts/train.py --config configs/default.yaml --exp-name my_run

See Quick Start for Python API usage, evaluation, and the phase transition sweep.

docs.title: Execution Agent
docs.text: The execution agent represents an institutional trader with a large position to execute over a finite horizon. It is the signal encoder — it transforms its private type into observable order flow while trying to minimise the information content of that flow.

docs.title: Observation Space
docs.text: Feature Description Normalisation LOB snapshot (80-dim) Bid/ask prices and sizes at 20 depth levels Prices: rel. to mid; sizes: max-norm exec_remaining Remaining inventory fraction / initial_inventory time_remaining Fraction of horizon left step / max_steps completion_frac Fraction of order executed 1 - remaining / initial mid_price Current mid price / 100.0 spread Current bid-ask spread / mid_price realized_vol Recent price volatility Annualised

docs.title: Action Space
docs.text: Dimension Range Meaning size_frac [0, 1] Fraction of max_order_size to trade this step limit_offset [-5, 5] Price offset from mid in ticks (negative = passive) order_type_logit [-1, 1] > 0 → limit; ≤ 0 → market order

docs.title: Reward
docs.text:
\[r_E = -\text{IS} - \lambda \cdot \hat{I}(z; F_t) - \mu \cdot \text{Predictability}(F_t) + r_\text{terminal}\]

Implementation Shortfall (IS): Arrival price × total quantity − Σ(fill_price × fill_qty). Measures how much worse than arrival price the execution achieved.

MI Penalty: MINE lower bound on I(z; F_t), estimated from the execution agent's flow feature buffer. Injected into the reward during training updates.

Terminal Penalty: Large penalty proportional to unexecuted inventory at horizon end, ensuring the agent does not trivially minimise MI by never trading.

docs.title: Market Maker Agent
docs.text: The market maker is the signal decoder — it provides liquidity and simultaneously performs real-time inference on the informational content of incoming order flow.

docs.title: Belief Model
docs.text:
The core innovation is an embedded Transformer encoder that processes the order flow sequence and outputs a calibrated belief:

\[b_t = P(z = \text{informed} \mid \mathcal{F}_t) \in [0, 1]\]

This belief is: 1. Directly appended to the market maker's observation before the policy network 2. Used to adjust spread and depth (wider spread when \(b_t\) is high) 3. Tracked as the primary detectability metric

docs.title: Observation Space
docs.text: Feature Description LOB snapshot (80-dim) Same as execution agent Flow history (60-dim) Last 20 fills encoded as (side, price_rel, size_rel) mm_inventory Current inventory / max inventory mid_price Current mid / 100 spread Current spread / mid belief Current b_t from transformer time_remaining Step fraction

docs.title: Action Space
docs.text: Dimension Range Meaning bid_offset [-spread_max, 0] Bid price below mid (in ticks) ask_offset [0, spread_max] Ask price above mid (in ticks) bid_size_frac [0, 1] Quote size on bid side ask_size_frac [0, 1] Quote size on ask side

docs.title: Reward
docs.text:
\[r_M = \text{PnL} - \alpha \cdot \text{AdvSel} - \beta \cdot H(b_t) + \gamma \cdot \text{Accuracy}(b_t)\]

The entropy regularisation \(-\beta H(b_t)\) is crucial: it prevents the market maker from becoming overconfident, which would make the policy brittle when the execution agent's strategy shifts.

docs.title: Latency Arbitrageur
docs.text: The arbitrageur is the temporal exploiter — it acts faster than the market maker can update its quotes, extracting profit from belief-update lag.

docs.title: Role in System Dynamics
docs.text:
Without the arbitrageur, the market maker could adopt a trivially safe strategy (always widen spreads). The arbitrageur makes this costly:

  • Wide spreads → stale quotes → sniping opportunities
  • This forces the market maker to solve a genuine inference problem

docs.title: Observation Space
docs.text: Feature Description LOB snapshot (80-dim) Current book state quote_staleness Proxy for how stale MM quotes are vs fair value arb_inventory Current position / max position mid_price Current mid / 100 time_remaining Step fraction

docs.title: Action Space
docs.text: Scalar \(a \in [-1, 1]\): - \(a > 0.33\): lift best ask (buy aggressively) - \(a < -0.33\): hit best bid (sell aggressively) - otherwise: do nothing

docs.title: Reward
docs.text:
\[r_A = \text{PnL}_\text{snipe} + \delta \cdot \text{BeliefLag}\]

The belief lag term rewards acting before the market maker's spread adjustment catches up to the new information — incentivising the arbitrageur to be maximally fast.

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text: See CHANGELOG.md in the repository root.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: All hyperparameters live in YAML files under configs/. Files inherit from default.yaml and override only the keys they change — no duplication.

docs.title: Config files
docs.text: File Purpose configs/default.yaml Full production config with all parameters documented configs/fast_debug.yaml Smoke-test config — tiny networks, 10-step episodes, CPU configs/ablation_lambda.yaml λ sweep config for phase transition analysis

docs.title: Key parameters
docs.text:
agents:
  execution:
    lambda_leakage: 0.5      # λ — MI penalty weight (the main experimental variable)
    mu_predictability: 0.1   # μ — flow predictability penalty weight
    inventory_lots: 100      # Total position to execute per episode
    horizon: 390             # Steps per episode

  market_maker:
    beta_entropy_reg: 0.05   # Prevents overconfident beliefs
    alpha_adverse_selection: 1.0
    gamma_belief_accuracy: 0.2

networks:
  belief_transformer:
    d_model: 128
    n_heads: 4
    n_layers: 4

training:
  total_timesteps: 10_000_000
  gamma: 0.99
  gae_lambda: 0.95
  clip_range: 0.2
  alternating:
    exec_phase_steps: 1000
    mm_arb_phase_steps: 1000

docs.title: CLI overrides
docs.text:
Any dot-path key can be overridden from the command line:

python scripts/train.py \
    --config configs/default.yaml \
    --override agents.execution.lambda_leakage=1.0 \
    --override training.total_timesteps=5000000 \
    --override device=cpu

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See CONTRIBUTING.md in the repository root for full details.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text:
# Setup
git clone https://github.com/yourusername/adversarial-market-marl.git
cd adversarial-market-marl
pip install -r requirements.txt && pip install -e .
pre-commit install

# Run all checks before pushing
black --target-version py310 --line-length 100 adversarial_market tests scripts
isort --profile black --line-length 100 adversarial_market tests scripts
flake8 adversarial_market scripts --max-line-length=100 --ignore=E203,W503
mypy adversarial_market --ignore-missing-imports --no-strict-optional
pytest tests/ -v

docs.title: Pull request checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] All 222 tests pass
  • [ ] Black, isort, and flake8 pass
  • [ ] mypy passes
  • [ ] New functionality has tests
  • [ ] Docstrings added for public methods

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Minimum Notes Python 3.10 3.11 also tested in CI PyTorch 2.1 CPU-only works; GPU strongly recommended RAM 16 GB 32 GB recommended for n_envs=8 CUDA (optional) 11.8 For GPU-accelerated training

docs.title: Standard install
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Nityahapani/adversarial-market-marl.git
cd adversarial-market-marl

python -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate        # Linux / macOS
# venv\Scripts\activate         # Windows

pip install -r requirements.txt
pip install -e .

# Verify
python -c "import adversarial_market; print('OK —', adversarial_market.__version__)"

docs.title: GPU setup
docs.text:
# CUDA 11.8
pip install torch==2.1.0+cu118 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118

# CUDA 12.1
pip install torch==2.1.0+cu121 --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

docs.title: Pre-commit hooks (contributors)
docs.text:
pip install pre-commit
pre-commit install

Hooks run automatically on git commit: black, isort, flake8, merge-conflict detection.

docs.title: Command-line training
docs.text:
# Fast smoke test — CPU, ~2 minutes
python scripts/train.py --config configs/fast_debug.yaml --exp-name debug

# Full run — GPU recommended
python scripts/train.py --config configs/default.yaml --exp-name my_run

# Override hyperparameters inline
python scripts/train.py \
    --config configs/default.yaml \
    --exp-name lambda_search \
    --override agents.execution.lambda_leakage=0.75

# Resume from checkpoint
python scripts/train.py \
    --config configs/default.yaml \
    --resume checkpoints/my_run/checkpoint_step_5000000.pt

docs.title: Python API
docs.text:
from adversarial_market.training.trainer import MARLTrainer
from adversarial_market.utils.config import load_config

config = load_config(
    "configs/default.yaml",
    overrides={"agents.execution.lambda_leakage": 0.5}
)
trainer = MARLTrainer(config)
trainer.train()

docs.title: Evaluation
docs.text:
python scripts/evaluate.py \
    --checkpoint checkpoints/my_run/checkpoint_final.pt \
    --n-episodes 20 \
    --output results/my_run/ \
    --plot

docs.title: Phase transition sweep
docs.text:
python scripts/sweep_lambda.py \
    --lambda-min 0.0 \
    --lambda-max 2.0 \
    --n-steps 20 \
    --seeds 3 \
    --output results/phase_transition/ \
    --plot

docs.title: Monitor with TensorBoard
docs.text: tensorboard --logdir runs/

docs.title: 1. Detectability Phase Transition
docs.text:
The central empirical result. As \(\lambda\) (leakage penalty) increases:

Metric Low λ (λ < λ*) High λ (λ > λ*) \(D_\text{KL}\)(informed || noise) High (> 1.0) Near zero (< 0.05) MM belief accuracy > 70% ≈ 50% (random guess) Implementation shortfall Low High (execution cost rises) Flow entropy Low (predictable) High (uniform, camouflaged) Average spread Tight Wide (MM uncertain)

Expected shape: KL divergence decreases sharply at \(\lambda^* \approx 0.5\)–\(1.0\) (model-dependent), with IS rising smoothly from zero.

docs.title: 2. Emergent Order Splitting
docs.text:
Without explicitly programming TWAP/VWAP, the execution agent should discover order splitting strategies through the reward signal alone. Evidence:

  • Order sizes should decrease with \(\lambda\) (smaller orders = less detectable)
  • Timing should become more uniform with \(\lambda\) (less burst trading)
  • Limit order fraction should increase with \(\lambda\) (passive orders look like noise)

docs.title: 3. Spread Widening Under Toxicity
docs.text:
The market maker should learn to widen spreads when \(b_t\) is high. Quantified by the spread-belief correlation:

\[\rho(\text{spread}_t, b_t) > 0\]

A well-trained market maker should show \(\rho > 0.6\) in low-\(\lambda\) regimes (where informed flow is detectable) and \(\rho \approx 0\) in high-\(\lambda\) regimes (where flow is camouflaged and the market maker cannot distinguish).

docs.title: 4. Latency Arbitrage Cycles
docs.text:
The arbitrageur should exhibit cyclical exploitation patterns: 1. Execution agent submits large order → price movement 2. Market maker belief updates with delay 3. Arbitrageur snipes stale quotes before spread adjustment 4. Market maker tightens, cycle resets

This cycle period should decrease as the market maker's belief transformer improves (faster belief updates = shorter exploitation window).

docs.title: 5. Breakdown of Detectability
docs.text:
The phase transition should manifest as a discontinuous drop in belief accuracy at \(\lambda^*\):

Belief Accuracy
    1.0 |████████████\
        |             \
    0.7 |              \_____________________
        |
    0.5 |─────────────────────────────────── (random)
        └──────────────────────────────────→ λ
        0    0.5   1.0   1.5   2.0
                    λ*

The sharp transition (as opposed to gradual) is the key theoretical prediction: it suggests the existence of a critical camouflage threshold analogous to phase transitions in statistical mechanics.

docs.title: 6. Training Stability Indicators
docs.text:
Healthy training should show:

  • Execution agent entropy: Initially high (random policy), then decreasing as it learns patterns, then increasing again as it discovers camouflage strategies
  • MM belief accuracy: Should initially increase (learning to classify), then decrease (execution agent adapts)
  • Alternating phase losses: Should decrease within each phase, with a small jump at phase switches (non-stationarity)
  • MINE estimate: Should track actual MI — rising when flow is predictable, falling when camouflage kicks in

docs.title: vs. TWAP Baseline
docs.text: A simple TWAP strategy trades fixed equal slices every period. Expected: - Lower IS than trained execution agent at low \(\lambda\) - Higher IS than trained agent at high \(\lambda\) (TWAP is trivially detectable)

docs.title: vs. Kyle Model Prediction
docs.text: The Kyle (1985) model predicts a linear information revelation schedule. The trained agent should match Kyle at \(\lambda = 0\) (no penalty) and deviate increasingly as \(\lambda\) rises.

docs.title: vs. No-Adversary Baseline
docs.text: Training execution agent alone (no MM inference, no arb) should produce lower IS but higher KL — confirming the adversarial pressure is necessary for camouflage emergence.

docs.title: 1. Market Microstructure as an Information Channel
docs.text: We model the limit order book as a noisy channel in the sense of Shannon (1948). The execution agent acts as an encoder, transforming its private information (latent type \(z \in \{0,1\}\)) into an observable signal (order flow \(F_t\)). The market maker acts as a decoder, performing sequential inference on \(F_t\) to recover \(z\).

docs.title: 1.1 The Kyle (1985) Baseline
docs.text:
Kyle's continuous auction model establishes that an informed trader optimally spreads their trades across time to minimise price impact. In equilibrium: - The market maker's pricing rule is linear in order flow - The informed trader's strategy is linear in their private signal - Information is incorporated gradually into prices

Our departure: Kyle's model is static and assumes closed-form optimal strategies. We replace both with learned policies in a dynamic adversarial game.

docs.title: 2.1 Mutual Information Penalty
docs.text:
The execution agent's reward is augmented with an information-theoretic penalty:

\[r_E = -\text{IS}(\pi_E) - \lambda \cdot \hat{I}(z; F_t) - \mu \cdot \text{Pred}(F_t)\]

where: - \(\text{IS}\) = implementation shortfall (execution cost) - \(\hat{I}(z; F_t)\) = estimated mutual information between the agent's type \(z\) and its observable order flow \(F_t\) - \(\text{Pred}(F_t)\) = predictability penalty on flow patterns - \(\lambda, \mu\) = trade-off weights

This creates a direct tension: executing the order (reducing IS) requires predictable, detectable flow; minimising leakage requires camouflaged, noisy flow.

docs.title: 2.2 MINE Lower Bound
docs.text:
Direct MI computation is intractable for continuous distributions. We use the MINE (Mutual Information Neural Estimation) lower bound:

\[I(X; Y) \geq \mathbb{E}_{p(x,y)}[T_\theta(x,y)] - \log\left(\mathbb{E}_{p(x)p(y)}[e^{T_\theta(x,y)}]\right)\]

where \(T_\theta: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y} \to \mathbb{R}\) is a learned statistics network. The bound is tight as \(T_\theta\) approaches the log density ratio.

We use the MINE-f variant with an exponential moving average baseline for variance reduction:

\[\hat{I}_\text{MINE-f} = \mathbb{E}_{p(x,y)}[T_\theta] - \frac{\mathbb{E}_{p(x)p(y)}[e^{T_\theta}]}{\bar{e}_t}\]

where \(\bar{e}_t = \alpha \bar{e}_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) \mathbb{E}[e^{T_\theta}]\).

docs.title: 3.1 Market Maker's Problem
docs.text:
The market maker performs Bayesian sequential inference:

\[b_t = P(z = 1 \mid \mathcal{F}_t)\]

where \(\mathcal{F}_t = \{(\text{side}_s, p_s, q_s, \tau_s)\}_{s \leq t}\) is the full order flow history.

By Bayes' rule:

\[b_t = \frac{f(F_t \mid z=1) \cdot b_{t-1}}{f(F_t \mid z=1) \cdot b_{t-1} + f(F_t \mid z=0) \cdot (1-b_{t-1})}\]

Rather than assuming parametric forms for \(f(\cdot \mid z)\), we learn the full mapping \(b_t = g_\phi(\mathcal{F}_t)\) end-to-end using a Transformer.

docs.title: 3.2 Belief Loss
docs.text:
The market maker's belief model is trained with a regularised binary cross-entropy:

\[\mathcal{L}_\phi = \text{BCE}(b_t, z) - \beta \cdot H(b_t)\]

The entropy term \(H(b_t) = -b_t \log b_t - (1-b_t)\log(1-b_t)\) penalises overconfidence, preventing the market maker from becoming brittle when the execution agent's strategy shifts.

docs.title: 4.1 Game Formulation
docs.text:
Define a three-player partially observable stochastic game (POSG):

\[\mathcal{G} = \langle \mathcal{S}, \{\mathcal{O}_i\}, \{\mathcal{A}_i\}, P, \{R_i\}, \gamma, T \rangle\]

where: - \(\mathcal{S}\) = global LOB state - \(\mathcal{O}_i\) = agent \(i\)'s partial observation - \(\mathcal{A}_i\) = agent \(i\)'s action space - \(P\) = LOB transition dynamics (price-time priority matching) - \(R_i\) = agent \(i\)'s reward function - \(T\) = episode horizon

A Nash equilibrium \((\pi_E^*, \pi_M^*, \pi_A^*)\) satisfies:

\[V_i(\pi_i^*, \pi_{-i}^*) \geq V_i(\pi_i, \pi_{-i}^*) \quad \forall \pi_i, \forall i\]

docs.title: 4.2 CTDE Framework
docs.text:
We use Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE): - During training: shared critic \(V(s, a_1, a_2, a_3)\) sees the full global state - During execution: each policy \(\pi_i(a_i \mid o_i)\) uses only its own observation

This is theoretically grounded in the centralised critic not needing to be deployed, so information sharing during training is permissible.

docs.title: 4.3 Alternating Optimisation
docs.text:
Simultaneous gradient updates in adversarial games are known to cause oscillation and divergence (simultaneous descent can cycle around Nash points). We use alternating optimisation:

Phase A (steps \(t \in [kT, kT + T_E)\)): - Update \(\pi_E\) with \(\pi_M, \pi_A\) frozen - Update \(T_\theta\) (MINE estimator)

Phase B (steps \(t \in [kT + T_E, (k+1)T)\)): - Update \(\pi_M, \pi_A\) with \(\pi_E\) frozen - Update \(g_\phi\) (belief transformer)

This mirrors the training of GANs and has convergence guarantees under mild smoothness conditions.

docs.title: 5.1 Formal Statement
docs.text:
Conjecture (Detectability Phase Transition): There exists a critical \(\lambda^* > 0\) such that:

\[\lim_{\lambda \to \lambda^*} D_\text{KL}(P_\text{informed}^{(\lambda)} \| P_\text{noise}) = 0\]

where \(P_\text{informed}^{(\lambda)}\) is the order flow distribution induced by the execution agent trained with leakage penalty \(\lambda\).

Below \(\lambda^*\): informed flow is statistically detectable (\(D_\text{KL} > 0\), market maker has accuracy \(> 0.5\)). Above \(\lambda^*\): informed flow is indistinguishable from noise (\(D_\text{KL} \approx 0\), market maker accuracy \(\approx 0.5\)).

docs.title: 5.2 The Cost-Detectability Trade-off
docs.text:
The trade-off is sharp: execution cost (IS) is monotonically increasing in \(\lambda\), while detectability is monotonically decreasing. The optimal \(\lambda\) depends on the agent's private valuation of information concealment vs execution quality.

\[\lambda^* = \arg\min_\lambda \{ \text{IS}(\lambda) + c \cdot D_\text{KL}(\lambda) \}\]

for some cost weight \(c > 0\) that encodes the regulatory or strategic penalty for detection.

docs.title: 6. The Latency Arbitrageur's Role
docs.text:
The arbitrageur introduces a second dimension of competition: time, in addition to information. It exploits the lag between: 1. New information arriving in the order flow 2. The market maker's belief update \(b_t\) 3. Quote adjustment by the market maker

Without the arbitrageur, the market maker could adopt a static "always wide spread" strategy that is immune to adverse selection. The arbitrageur punishes excessively wide spreads by sniping them, forcing the market maker to solve a joint problem:

\[\min_{\text{spread}} \; \text{AdvSel}(\text{spread}) + \text{Snipe Risk}(\text{spread})\]

which admits an interior optimum requiring genuine inference rather than a corner solution.

docs.title: adversarial_market.agents
docs.text: Agent wrapper classes providing the BaseAgent interface. Each class wraps one or more network modules and handles episode-level state tracking.

docs.title: adversarial_market.agents.base_agent.BaseAgent
docs.text:
Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for execution agent, market maker, and arbitrageur.

Subclasses implement

build_networks() → construct policy and auxiliary networks observe(state) → extract own observation from global MarketState act(obs) → sample action from policy; return (action, log_prob, entropy) update(batch) → run gradient update; return metrics dict

Attributes:

Name Type Description agent_id

Integer ID (0=exec, 1=mm, 2=arb).

name

Human-readable agent name.

device

Torch device for network tensors.

config

Agent-specific config sub-dict.

docs.title: build_networks() abstractmethod
docs.text: Construct and register all neural network modules.

docs.title: act(obs, deterministic=False) abstractmethod
docs.text:
Sample an action from the current policy.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obs ndarray

Observation vector (numpy, already on CPU).

required deterministic bool

If True, return mode rather than sample (for eval).

False

Returns:

Name Type Description action ndarray

Numpy action array.

log_prob float

Log probability of the sampled action (scalar float).

entropy float

Policy entropy at this observation (scalar float).

docs.title: update(batch) abstractmethod
docs.text:
Perform a gradient update step on the given minibatch.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default batch Dict[str, Tensor]

Dict containing tensors for obs, actions, advantages, returns, etc.

required

Returns:

Type Description Dict[str, float]

Dict of scalar metric values for logging.

docs.title: register_network(net)
docs.text: Register a network module so it is tracked for freezing/parameter counting.

docs.title: freeze()
docs.text: Freeze all registered network parameters (no gradient updates).

docs.title: unfreeze()
docs.text: Unfreeze all registered network parameters.

docs.title: parameter_count()
docs.text: Total number of trainable parameters across all registered networks.

docs.title: save_state(path)
docs.text: Save all network state dicts to a single file.

docs.title: load_state(path)
docs.text: Load network state dicts from file.

docs.title: adversarial_market.agents.execution_agent.ExecutionAgent
docs.text:
Bases: BaseAgent

Institutional execution agent.

Maintains
  • Actor policy π_E(a | o_E)
  • MINE estimator for leakage measurement
  • Predictability penalty network
  • Episode-level tracking (inventory, fills, IS)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default config Dict[str, Any]

Full agent config dict (agents.execution section).

required net_cfg Dict[str, Any]

Network architecture config dict (networks section).

required device str

Torch device string.

'cpu'

docs.title: estimate_leakage()
docs.text: Compute current MI leakage estimate from flow buffer.

docs.title: adversarial_market.agents.market_maker.MarketMakerAgent
docs.text:
Bases: BaseAgent

Adaptive market maker with embedded sequential belief inference.

Maintains a rolling window of order flow observations and passes them through the BeliefTransformer to compute b_t = P(informed | F_t).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default config Dict[str, Any]

agents.market_maker config section.

required net_cfg Dict[str, Any]

networks config section.

required obs_dim int

Actor observation dimension.

required device str

Torch device string.

'cpu'

docs.title: observe_flow_event(side, price_rel, size_rel, order_type, time_frac)
docs.text: Add a new order flow event to the rolling window.

docs.title: update_belief()
docs.text: Recompute b_t from the current flow window. Returns the updated belief scalar.

docs.title: implied_spread_multiplier()
docs.text:
Returns a multiplier in [1, 3] that widens the spread proportionally to the current belief b_t.

Rational MM: spread ∝ adverse selection risk ∝ P(informed). Derived from Glosten-Milgrom (1985): spread = 2 * b_t * E[|v - p| | informed] / (1 - b_t + ε) We approximate with a simple linear scale.

docs.title: adversarial_market.agents.arbitrageur.ArbitrageAgent
docs.text:
Bases: BaseAgent

Latency arbitrageur.

Exploits quote staleness and belief-update lag to extract profit from the market maker. Its presence prevents degenerate equilibria.

Action interpretation

a > +0.33 → lift best ask (buy, expecting upward correction) a < -0.33 → hit best bid (sell, expecting downward correction) otherwise → do nothing

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default config Dict[str, Any]

agents.arbitrageur config section.

required net_cfg Dict[str, Any]

networks config section.

required obs_dim int

Actor observation dimension.

required device str

Torch device string.

'cpu'

docs.title: compute_quote_staleness(best_bid, best_ask, mid_price, tick_size, fair_spread_ticks=2.0) staticmethod
docs.text:
Compute a staleness score ∈ [0, 5] for current MM quotes.

Staleness = how much wider the current spread is versus the fair (competitive) spread. Wider than fair → MM has not yet adjusted quotes to new information → sniping opportunity.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default best_bid Optional[float]

Current best bid price (or None).

required best_ask Optional[float]

Current best ask price (or None).

required mid_price float

Current mid-point price.

required tick_size float

Minimum price increment.

required fair_spread_ticks float

Expected competitive spread in ticks.

2.0

Returns:

Type Description float

Staleness score clipped to [0, 5].

docs.title: should_trade(action_scalar)
docs.text: Interpret scalar action as a trading decision.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment
docs.text: The limit order book simulation environment.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment.lob_env.LOBEnvironment
docs.text:
Bases: Env

Multi-agent limit order book environment.

Observation spaces (per agent): Execution agent : [lob_snapshot | own_state | time_features] Market maker : [lob_snapshot | flow_history | own_state | belief] Arbitrageur : [lob_snapshot | quote_staleness | own_state]

Action spaces

Execution agent : Box([order_size, limit_offset, order_type_logit]) Market maker : Box([bid_offset_ticks, ask_offset_ticks, bid_size, ask_size]) Arbitrageur : Discrete (do nothing | hit bid | lift ask)

The environment produces observations, rewards, and done flags for all three agents. The trainer coordinates the action loop.

docs.title: step(actions)
docs.text:
Process one environment step.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default actions Dict[str, ndarray]

{"execution": array, "market_maker": array, "arbitrageur": array}

required

Returns:

Type Description Any

obs, rewards, terminated, truncated, info (all dicts keyed by agent name)

docs.title: get_flow_buffer()
docs.text: Return raw flow feature buffer for external MINE estimation.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment.order_book.OrderBook
docs.text:
Central limit order book with price-time priority matching.

Price levels
  • Bids sorted descending (best bid = highest price)
  • Asks sorted ascending (best ask = lowest price)
Matching algorithm

For each incoming order, walk through resting levels on the opposite side in price-time priority until the order is filled or no more matching quotes exist. Limit orders that are not fully matched rest at their price level.

docs.title: submit(order)
docs.text: Submit an order. Returns list of Fill events generated. Market orders fill immediately or are cancelled if no liquidity. Limit orders rest if not immediately matchable.

docs.title: cancel(order_id)
docs.text: Cancel a resting order. Returns True if found and cancelled.

docs.title: step()
docs.text: Advance internal clock by one step.

docs.title: get_snapshot(depth=10)
docs.text: Returns LOB snapshot as numpy array of shape (depth * 4,): [bid_price_1, bid_size_1, ..., bid_price_depth, bid_size_depth, ask_price_1, ask_size_1, ..., ask_price_depth, ask_size_depth] Prices normalised by current mid; sizes normalised by max observable.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment.order.Order dataclass
docs.text:
Represents a single order submitted to the LOB.

Attributes:

Name Type Description order_id int

Unique identifier.

agent_id int

Which agent submitted this order (0=exec, 1=mm, 2=arb, 3=noise).

side Side

BID (buy) or ASK (sell).

order_type OrderType

MARKET or LIMIT.

price float

Limit price (ignored for market orders).

size int

Original order quantity in lots.

remaining int

Unfilled quantity in lots.

timestamp int

Submission time (env step counter).

status OrderStatus

Current order status.

fills list[tuple[float, int]]

List of (price, quantity) tuples from partial/full fills.

docs.title: apply_fill(fill_price, fill_qty)
docs.text: Update order state after a fill event.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment.order.Fill dataclass
docs.text: Record of a matched trade.

docs.title: adversarial_market.environment.market_state.MarketState dataclass
docs.text:
Complete market state snapshot passed to agents each step.

Contains
  • LOB snapshot (normalised numpy array)
  • Recent fills (order flow visible to market maker)
  • Per-agent state (inventory, PnL, etc.)
  • Mid price, spread, volatility estimates
  • Step counter and episode info

docs.title: to_array()
docs.text: Compact numpy representation of global market state (for critic).

docs.title: adversarial_market.evaluation
docs.text: Policy evaluation and result analysis.

docs.title: adversarial_market.evaluation.evaluator.Evaluator
docs.text:
Runs n_episodes of evaluation using provided trained policies.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default config Dict[str, Any]

Full configuration dict.

required device str

Torch device string.

'cpu'

docs.title: evaluate(exec_actor, mm_actor, arb_actor, belief_transformer, mine, n_episodes=10, noise_only_episodes=5, seed=None)
docs.text:
Run evaluation and return summary metrics dict.

Also runs noise-only episodes to compute KL divergence between informed and noise flow distributions.

docs.title: adversarial_market.evaluation.metrics
docs.text:
Evaluation metrics for the adversarial market MARL framework.

Key metrics
  • Implementation shortfall (IS): execution cost vs arrival price
  • KL divergence: D_KL(informed_flow || noise_flow) — detectability measure
  • Market maker belief accuracy: how accurately MM classifies informed flow
  • Spread dynamics: how spread responds to perceived toxicity
  • Leakage score: mutual information estimate at evaluation time

docs.title: implementation_shortfall(arrival_price, fill_prices, fill_quantities, side=1)
docs.text:
Implementation shortfall = signed cost above arrival price.

For a buy order: IS = sum(fill_price * qty) / sum(qty) - arrival_price Higher IS = worse execution.

docs.title: kl_divergence_flows(informed_flows, noise_flows, n_bins=20)
docs.text:
Estimate D_KL(P_informed || P_noise) over order flow features.

Uses histogram-based KL divergence on each feature dimension, then averages (simplification; for full multivariate KL use kernel methods).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default informed_flows ndarray

(N, F) feature matrix from execution agent episodes

required noise_flows ndarray

(M, F) feature matrix from noise-trader-only episodes

required

Returns:

Type Description float

Scalar KL divergence estimate. Near 0 = indistinguishable flows.

docs.title: belief_accuracy(beliefs, true_labels, threshold=0.5)
docs.text:
Classification accuracy of market maker's belief b_t.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default beliefs ndarray

(T,) predicted probabilities in [0,1]

required true_labels ndarray

(T,) binary 0/1 ground truth

required threshold float

Decision boundary

0.5

Returns:

Type Description float

Fraction of correctly classified steps.

docs.title: flow_entropy(flow_features, n_bins=10)
docs.text:
Empirical entropy of order flow distribution.

Higher entropy = more uniform / less predictable flow pattern. This is the quantity the execution agent tries to maximise.

docs.title: compute_summary_metrics(episodes)
docs.text: Aggregate metrics across multiple evaluation episodes.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks
docs.text: Neural network modules used by the three agents.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.belief_transformer.BeliefTransformer
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Transformer encoder that processes order flow history and outputs a belief scalar b_t = P(flow is informed | F_t).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default input_dim int

Dimension of each order flow event feature vector.

5 d_model int

Transformer hidden dimension.

128 n_heads int

Number of attention heads.

4 n_layers int

Number of encoder layers.

4 d_ff int

Feed-forward sublayer hidden dimension.

256 dropout float

Dropout probability.

0.1 max_seq_len int

Maximum sequence length (history window).

200

docs.title: forward(flow_seq, src_key_padding_mask=None)
docs.text:
Parameters:

Name Type Description Default flow_seq Tensor

(B, T, input_dim) order flow event sequence

required src_key_padding_mask Optional[Tensor]

(B, T) bool mask — True = padding position

None

Returns:

Name Type Description belief Tensor

(B,) scalar in [0, 1] — P(informed | F_t)

repr Tensor

(B, d_model//4) latent representation of the flow

docs.title: compute_belief_loss(flow_seq, labels, src_key_padding_mask=None, entropy_reg_weight=0.05)
docs.text:
Supervised belief loss for market maker training.

Labels = 1 if the flow in this episode came from the execution agent (informed), 0 if pure noise.

Loss = BCE(belief, label) - beta * H(belief) The entropy regularisation prevents overconfident beliefs.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default flow_seq Tensor

(B, T, input_dim)

required labels Tensor

(B,) binary 0/1

required src_key_padding_mask Optional[Tensor]

(B, T)

None entropy_reg_weight float

beta in the reward formula

0.05

Returns:

Name Type Description loss Tensor

scalar training loss

belief Tensor

(B,) predicted beliefs

docs.title: update_belief(flow_seq, src_key_padding_mask=None)
docs.text: Online belief update — inference only.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.mine_estimator.MINEEstimator
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Full MINE wrapper with exponential moving average baseline for variance reduction (MINE-f variant from Belghazi et al.).

Usage

estimator = MINEEstimator(z_dim=1, f_dim=4) mi_lower_bound, loss = estimator.compute(z_samples, f_samples) loss.backward() # update the statistics network

The MI lower bound is used as a penalty term in the execution agent's reward: r_E += -lambda_leakage * mi_lower_bound

docs.title: compute(z, f)
docs.text:
Estimate MI(z; f) and return (mi_estimate, loss).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default z Tensor

shape (B, z_dim) — joint samples (z_i, f_i drawn together)

required f Tensor

shape (B, f_dim) — marginal shuffle applied inside

required

Returns:

Name Type Description mi_estimate Tensor

scalar lower bound on I(z; f)

loss Tensor

negative DV bound (minimise to tighten the estimate)

docs.title: estimate_only(z, f)
docs.text: Fast inference-only MI estimate (no gradient tracking).

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.actor_critic.ExecutionActor
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Execution agent stochastic policy π_E(a | o_E).

Action: [size_frac, limit_offset, order_type_logit] Uses Beta distribution for bounded continuous actions (size_frac, limit_offset_norm) and Bernoulli for discrete order type.

docs.title: forward(obs)
docs.text:
Returns:

Name Type Description action Tensor

(B, 3) sampled action

log_prob Tensor

(B,) log probability

entropy Tensor

(B,) policy entropy

docs.title: evaluate_actions(obs, actions)
docs.text: Evaluate log prob and entropy of given actions (for PPO update).

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.actor_critic.MarketMakerActor
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Market maker stochastic policy π_M(a | o_M, b_t).

Action: [bid_offset, ask_offset, bid_size_frac, ask_size_frac] The belief b_t is appended to the observation before passing through the network, so spread decisions are directly conditioned on inference.

Uses Normal distribution with learned log_std.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.actor_critic.ArbitrageActor
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Arbitrageur stochastic policy π_A(a | o_A).

Action: scalar ∈ [-1, 1] — direction and intensity of aggression. Uses tanh-squashed Normal distribution.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.actor_critic.SharedCritic
docs.text:
Bases: Module

Centralized value function V(s) for MAPPO.

Input: concatenation of global market state + all agent observations. Output: scalar value estimate.

docs.title: forward(global_state)
docs.text: Returns value estimate, shape (B,).

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.execution_policy.TWAPPolicy
docs.text:
Bases: BaseExecutionPolicy

Time-Weighted Average Price (TWAP) execution baseline.

Splits the total order into equal-sized slices and trades one slice per time step. Submits market orders to guarantee fill.

Properties
  • Maximally predictable (constant order size every step)
  • Minimal IS in liquid markets with flat volume profile
  • KL(TWAP || noise) is high — easily detectable

Used as the lower bound on KL divergence in phase transition analysis.

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.execution_policy.VWAPPolicy
docs.text:
Bases: BaseExecutionPolicy

Volume-Weighted Average Price (VWAP) execution baseline.

Targets a volume profile derived from a typical intraday U-shape (high activity at open/close, lower mid-day). Sizes orders to match the expected fraction of daily volume in each period.

Properties
  • More sophisticated than TWAP — adapts to volume pattern
  • Still deterministic and partially predictable
  • KL(VWAP || noise) < KL(TWAP || noise) but > 0

docs.title: adversarial_market.networks.execution_policy.AdaptiveCamouflagePolicy
docs.text:
Bases: BaseExecutionPolicy

Hybrid policy that blends noise and execution orders for camouflage.

At each step, with probability p_noise, submits a random noise-like order (small size, random offset, random type) instead of the actual execution order. This explicit mixing is a hand-crafted baseline for the learned camouflage strategy.

Used to verify that the learned policy discovers something beyond simple noise injection.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default base_policy BaseExecutionPolicy

Underlying execution policy.

required p_noise float

Probability of substituting a noise order.

0.3 rng_seed int

Random seed for reproducibility.

0

docs.title: adversarial_market.training
docs.text: MAPPO training infrastructure.

docs.title: adversarial_market.training.trainer.MARLTrainer
docs.text:
End-to-end MAPPO trainer for the adversarial market MARL system.

Components
  • LOBEnvironment: single-agent step interface (multi-agent coordinated here)
  • ExecutionActor, MarketMakerActor, ArbitrageActor: agent policies
  • SharedCritic: centralized value function
  • BeliefTransformer: MM's sequential inference model
  • MINEEstimator: MI lower bound for execution agent reward shaping
  • PredictabilityPenalty: flow pattern detection penalty
  • AlternatingOptimizer: phase scheduler
  • RolloutBuffer: GAE-λ rollout storage

docs.title: train()
docs.text: Run the full training loop.

docs.title: adversarial_market.training.alternating_opt.AlternatingOptimizer
docs.text:
Controls which agents are active (receiving gradient updates) each step.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default exec_phase_steps int

Number of environment steps per execution phase.

1000 mm_arb_phase_steps int

Number of environment steps per MM+Arb phase.

1000 start_phase Phase

Which phase to start in.

EXECUTION

docs.title: active_agents property
docs.text: Return the list of agent names that should be updated this phase.

docs.title: step(n_steps=1)
docs.text: Advance the scheduler by n_steps and switch phase if needed.

docs.title: phase_progress()
docs.text: Fraction of current phase completed.

docs.title: adversarial_market.training.rollout_buffer.RolloutBuffer
docs.text:
Stores multi-agent rollout transitions and computes GAE advantages.

Usage

buffer = RolloutBuffer(rollout_length=2048, n_envs=8, gamma=0.99, gae_lambda=0.95)

docs.title: ... collect transitions ...
docs.text: buffer.add(obs_dict, action_dict, logp_dict, reward_dict, value_dict, done_dict) buffer.compute_returns_and_advantages(last_values_dict) for batch in buffer.get_minibatches(minibatch_size=256): # PPO update

docs.title: add(obs, global_state, actions, log_probs, rewards, values, dones, entropies)
docs.text: Add one timestep of transitions (all envs batched).

docs.title: compute_returns_and_advantages(last_values)
docs.text:
Compute GAE-λ advantages and discounted returns for each agent.

GAE: A_t = Σ_{l=0}^{∞} (γλ)^l δ_{t+l} where δ_t = r_t + γ * V(s_{t+1}) * (1-d_t) - V(s_t)

docs.title: get_minibatches(minibatch_size)
docs.text: Yield random minibatches of transitions for PPO updates. Each minibatch contains tensors for all agents.

docs.title: adversarial_market.training.ppo_update.PPOUpdate
docs.text:
Single-agent PPO update step.

Clipped objective

L_CLIP = E[min(r_t * A_t, clip(r_t, 1-ε, 1+ε) * A_t)] r_t = π_new(a|s) / π_old(a|s)

Total loss

L = -L_CLIP + c_v * L_VF - c_e * H(π)

docs.title: update(obs, global_states, actions, log_probs_old, advantages, returns, values_old, extra_reward=None)
docs.text:
Perform one PPO minibatch update.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default obs Tensor

Agent-specific observations, (B, obs_dim)

required global_states Tensor

Global state for critic, (B, global_dim)

required actions Tensor

Taken actions, (B, action_dim)

required log_probs_old Tensor

Log probs under old policy, (B,)

required advantages Tensor

GAE advantages, (B,)

required returns Tensor

Discounted returns, (B,)

required values_old Tensor

Old value estimates, (B,)

required extra_reward Optional[Tensor]

Optional additional scalar reward (e.g., -MI penalty)

None

Returns:

Type Description Dict[str, float]

Dictionary of scalar metrics for logging.

docs.title: adversarial_market.utils.math_utils
docs.text:
Mathematical utilities for information-theoretic computations.

All functions are numpy-based for use outside the training loop. Torch-based equivalents live in the MINE estimator module.

docs.title: compute_kl_divergence(p, q, epsilon=1e-08)
docs.text:
Discrete KL divergence D_KL(P || Q).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default p ndarray

probability distribution (will be normalised)

required q ndarray

probability distribution (will be normalised)

required epsilon float

smoothing constant to avoid log(0)

1e-08

Returns:

Type Description float

Scalar KL divergence in nats.

docs.title: compute_entropy(p, epsilon=1e-08)
docs.text: Shannon entropy H(P) in nats.

docs.title: empirical_entropy(samples, n_bins=20)
docs.text:
Estimate H(X) from samples using histogram density estimation.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default samples ndarray

1-D array of observations

required n_bins int

number of histogram bins

20

Returns:

Type Description float

Entropy estimate in nats.

docs.title: mutual_information_histogram(x, y, n_bins=20)
docs.text:
Estimate I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y) via joint histogram.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default x ndarray

(N,) samples

required y ndarray

(N,) samples (same length as x)

required n_bins int

bins per dimension

20

Returns:

Type Description float

MI estimate in nats.

docs.title: jensen_shannon_divergence(p, q, epsilon=1e-08)
docs.text:
Jensen-Shannon divergence (symmetric, bounded in [0, ln2]).

JSD(P||Q) = 0.5 * KL(P || M) + 0.5 * KL(Q || M) where M = 0.5 * (P + Q)

docs.title: total_variation_distance(p, q)
docs.text: TV distance: 0.5 * sum |p_i - q_i|.

0.99 lam float
